settings.title: What to Actually Wear for Portraits
settings.excerpt: The clothes you choose matter more than the camera we bring, and the rules are simpler than you think.
settings.body:
The question we hear most before a shoot is what everyone should wear. After years behind the lens, our advice has gotten shorter, not longer.

Stick to a small palette. Two or three colours that sit well together read far better than everyone in their favourite shirt.

Skip tight patterns and big logos, which fight for attention against your face. And wear something you can move in, because the best frames almost always happen between the posed ones.

A simple way to plan it

Pick one person's outfit first, usually whoever is fussiest about clothes, then build everyone else around those colours. You are not trying to match exactly, just to stay in the same family of tones so no single person jumps out of the photo.

  • Lay every outfit on a bed together before the day.
  • Choose soft, solid colours over bright whites or deep blacks.
  • Iron the night before, since creases show more than you expect.

That fifteen minutes of planning saves a lot of second-guessing on the day, and it means we can spend the session on the people rather than the clothes.

What matters more than any of this

Comfort beats every rule above. A child who hates a stiff collar will tell you about it in every frame, so let them wear something they forget they have on.

And do not overthink it. The photos people keep are rarely the perfectly styled ones, they are the ones where everybody looks like themselves. Dress for that, and we will handle the rest.
